sentences.text: That's professional.
Yes.
He took a lot of pictures that were used in trade magazines.
Yeah, when I was in Kenya, I-- The date is December 20, 1995.
The survivor is Hans Hammelbacher.
I'm Sherry Amatenstein.
We're in Forest Hills, New York in the US.
And we're speaking today in English.
My name is Hans Hammelbacher.
H-A-N-S H-A-M-M-E-L-B-A-C-H-E-R.
I was born January 6, 1911.
I'm 84, pushing 85.
Born in Nuremberg, Germany, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Bavaria.
That's where I grew up.
I spent my childhood there, and then I went away to college, and then I came back again until we finally emigrated.
My father was in the hops business.
He sold hops to breweries.
He also owned a brewery in Lower Bavaria, which was several hours away from Nuremberg.
And we divided our time between the two cities, especially my father, who had to look after the business at the brewery.
In the summer, we spent about six weeks' vacation down at the brewery, usually, where we had a nice house and a big garden with lots of vegetables and fruit trees.
Everything grew right there.
The happiest memories were, believe it or not, of school.
I had a very good time in school, first in four years of preliminary school and then nine years of gymnasium, which is the high school.
And the last two years of gymnasium are really the equivalent of the first two years of college in this country.
And it was a nice time.
We had good friends.
The family was closely-knit.
And it was really a carefree childhood.
My hobby was reading.
I usually read two books simultaneously per week.
And sports, I was not the greatest in.
I played some tennis, and I did some swimming.
But I preferred to lie on, on the couch and read.
Yes.
I had a good voice, if I may say so.
And later on, I took voice lessons while I was studying in Munich.
That was my second occupation there.
And I got to the point where I would have been ready to go on stage.
I had a tenor voice, lyric voice.
I wouldn't have made a heldentenor, but of course, the Nazis made all this impossible.
Yes.
There was one big temple in Nuremberg which was the-- called the Liberal Temple, which was the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mixture between Conservative and Reform.
And the other temple was Orthodox, which we did not belong to.
My father came from an Orthodox house, and we belonged to the-- but we belonged to the liberal temple together with all our friends.
In fact, my wife's parents belonged to the same temple.
Yes, I did.
Spent all Friday nights, Saturday mornings, and, of course, the holidays at the temple.
And we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were married at the temple.
At that time, the temple had a, a little annex for smaller occasions.
We could not use the main synagogue anymore in 1937--
OK.
They were always occasions where we went to temple, where we stayed at home, and it was always festive.
And no school during that time, which at times made it difficult, because school went on for the rest of the class.
We were only four Jewish boys in our class.
The rest were all Gentile.
And so the classes went on, and we had to make up for lost time.
There was very good food all year around, really.
And we usually had a cook, a maid, who cooked at the same time.
At one time we had a professional gourmet cook as a maid, and that was a wonderful time.
We still have a few recipes from that time, which are delicious.
Nuremberg was a city of 320,000 inhabitants and 9,000 Jews.
Not in the earlier years, but by the time I was 15 years old, anti-Semitism started to grow.
You had the Nazi slogans all over with swastikas painted on the walls or written in chalk.
We would always try to erase them or complete a, a square by filling in the, the missing lines of the swastika.
But later on, that became impossible.
You couldn't desecrate the swastikas anymore, so to speak.
And as I grew older, anti-Semitism became a real factor.
At one time I went to school, maybe I was 16 years old.
I met another boy.
He came along and said, saujude, pig of a Jew.
And I reached out and whacked him.
And we got into a brawl until we were finally separated.
In later years, that would have been impossible on my part.
I could not have done that.
It was even risky at that time, but it was an instantaneous reaction.
No, we did not.
We were not Zionist-oriented in our family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We considered ourselves Jewish-- German citizens of the Jewish religion.
And we considered ourselves good Germans who happened to be Jewish.
Our families had been rooted, rooted in Germany for so many years.
I can trace my family tree back to about 1680 so far, trying to get further back.
And they had always lived in Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were very much part of-- we, we considered ourselves part of the German scene.
We learned better later on.
At home?
German.
Only German.
I learned [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Latin in school for nine years and Greek for six years, ancient Greek.
And then I had Italian as an, as an elective and later on, in this country, Spanish.
I went to school at night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, we had Hebrew lessons in school.
There were so few Jews in, in school that several classes were taken together to make up one religious class.
And since there was no separation of church and state in Germany, religious instructions were always held on a certain day at a certain time.
The Catholics went to their class, the Protestants to another, and the Jews to theirs.
And the Jews came from several grades because there wouldn't have been enough otherwise.
Yes.
In the beginning, quite a, quite a few.
Later on, it took an act of courage to be friendly to a Jew.
It became so that in the last two grades in high school, the Jews-- we were four of us-- were completely isolated.
Nobody would associate with us.
During intermission, we were left to ourselves.
No, they didn't.
They just stayed away.
They wouldn't even look at us anymore.
They-- somebody whom I was particularly friendly with, I met him in the street.
He looked the other way.
One classmate [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] remained faithful to the very end, until we left.
He took a big chance.
And I'm sure he was probably reprimanded for it by the Nazis.
[NON-ENGLISH].
And unfortunately, he died very young.
I went to college in 1930.
First I went to the University of Munich to study law.
After one term, I had permission to spend several terms abroad.
You needed special permission.
If your grades were good enough, you got it.
So I spent one term at the London School of Economics, which was London University.
And immediately afterwards, I went to Paris to the Sorbonne and studied one term there.
Then I went back to Munich and had to make up for a lot of lost time in a hurry.
And in 1933, I took my first exam, which would have been followed, two years later, by the final exam.
The system's a little different from the one we have here.
So I passed the preliminary exam, which was called the [NON-ENGLISH] exam.
After that, you spend a year at court as a court clerk.
And then you went on with your studies.
I could not do that anymore because that was in the spring of '33, just after the Nazis had come to power.
That was the end of my legal career.
I could not continue.
Nobody would accept me anymore.
As a Jew, no university-- I tried-- but they wouldn't accept me.
I tried the, the University of Erlangen, which is a small city within 20 miles of Nuremberg.
I tried the University of Wurzberg, which is also not too far away.
I tried Berlin.
I was not accepted by anybody.

So the final exam at the University of Munich was already most difficult, because instead of a legal question, in this particular department we were asked to innumerate the mistakes and faults of the Weimar Republic.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I helped myself because, as a Jew, they knew that my sympathies lay with the Weimar Republic, not with the Nazis, which had, had followed the Weimar Republic regime.
So I helped myself by saying, in the light of today's prevailing opinion, the faults of the Weimar Republic were the following.
This way I could repeat all the Nazi accusations against the Weimar Republic without identifying myself with those accusations.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was an extremely difficult time, but it ended my legal career right then and there.
What were the, the changes that you started noticing once Hitler came to power in '33?
How did things get more and more difficult?
Well, things became more and more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] outspokenly anti-Semitic.
In Nuremberg we had a virulent anti-Semite, Julius Streicher, who was hanged after the Nuremberg Trials.
He was condemned to death and hanged.
He published a paper which was the vilest, most vulgar magazine you ever saw in your life against the Jews.
was the name of that magazine.
And it always depicted Jews with huge hooked noses and, you know, the real anti-Semitic Jewish type, the ones as the anti-Semites painted it.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, your friends, former friends wouldn't look at you anymore.
And the, the papers became more and more filled with anti-Semitic articles, and we felt more and more uncomfortable.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The climax was reached at Kristallnacht.
OK.
We were very slow in realizing that we had to get out of Germany.
As I said before, we had that brewery down in, in the country where everybody was friendly.
And everybody reassured us, oh, this is not going to last.
People like you are not affected.
If Hitler knew what was going on, he would stop it immediately.
And things like that.
Of course, that we never believed for one minute, but we were optimistic and thought it would blow over.
It couldn't last.
So we were very late in realizing that we had to get out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One event made it very clear, finally.
My father was accused of Rassenschande, having a relationship with a Gentile woman, which was a completely trumped-up charge.
He was taken to the concentration camp of Dachau.
That was in 1937.
Hella and I had just gotten married.
And in those days, it was still possible.
People still got out of, of Dachau.
He was there for about a week, four, five days, I guess.
Hella and I, we drove to Munich where the headquarters of the Nazi, of SS, the Stormtroopers, was.
And we spoke to somebody there in full uniform.
In later years, all this would have been impossible.
And we pleaded our case and succeeded.
After about four or five days, my father was let go.
That this must be an error.
We would like to know who accused him of that.
And we tried to prove it couldn't be possible.
We had witnesses who did testify that this was most, almost impossible, because this was a village down in the country, and everybody would have known what was going on.
So they finally let him go.
He never said one word about his experiences at Dachau.
Not one word.
Just kept silent about it.
As I said, in those days, we knew several other men who were taken to Dachau and, and set free again.
But later on, that became a different story.
We're not sure when we met first.
It was probably on a tennis court in Nuremberg, and-- but didn't pay much attention, much attention to each other.
We finally got to know us better on a vacation where my wife was together with friends, with her father as a chaperone.
And I was at the University of Munich at that time.
And a, a friend of mine was there, too.
And he knew that this whole group was vacationing somewhere in Switzerland.
And he said, why don't we crash the party and go over there?
So I went with him reluctantly.
And that's where we really got to know each other and fell in love, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we finally became engaged.
How long did it take before you became engaged?
It took another [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three, three, four years.
The engagement was rather dramatic.
Hella was ready to emigrate to Argentina to join a cousin of hers with his wife who had gone there previously.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just couldn't bear the idea of having her go away.
The closer the departure date came, the more I realized that I just couldn't let it happen.
So that's when I asked her to marry me, and she said yes.
She literally had to unpack her suitcases.
Not a figure of speech.
She was, she was ready to leave.
So we became engaged and got married in Nuremberg.
At that synagogue, you said?
At the synagogue.
It was a small affair, because of the circumstances.
By that time, it was 1937, and you had to be as inconspicuous as possible.
You showed yourself as little as possible in the street.
You couldn't have a nice wedding ceremony at the temple.
In fact, the big temple was closed.
It was not used, and only the smaller little temple which was next door was in use.
You tried to be as inconspicuous as possible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as I said, you showed yourself in the street as little as possible.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Nuremberg was-- even though it was a city of almost 400,000, it was still a small town, and everybody knew everybody else, more or less.
And especially the Jews were known.
And the Germans knew exactly who was Jewish and who was not.
Well, nothing really before before Kristallnacht.
That's when things started to become physically dangerous.

At that time, since the legal career was closed to me and singing also-- the singing career was also closed-- I joined my father's business and took care of the hops business and the brewery, to a far lesser extent.
That really was my father's department, but I was in the hops business, together with a cousin of mine whose father had been part owner.

And so we two were in the office together, and, together with my father, ran the business, which became more and more difficult because the Nazis made it almost impossible to conduct a business for Jews.
They, well, they came up in uniform, the SS, the SA, with all kinds of questions, and it just became extremely uncomfortable.
They would ask you about what you were doing, how you conducted your business.
They wanted to be sure that you didn't smuggle any money abroad, so they kept you under supervision.
And it became almost impossible.
Well, it was a terrible situation, and in the end we realized that the only thing to do was to leave Germany.
But by that time, it was extremely difficult.
You need-- in order to get-- all the Jewish passports were taken away.
You could not have a passport unless you paid the so-called Reichsfluchtsteuer, literally Reich Flight Tax.
In other words, you had to pay a substantial part of your fortune, I think it was 60%, to the Nazi party or to the Reich in order to get permission to leave Germany.
They called it Reich Flight Tax, which was ironic because this was a forced exodus.
Now, our money was tied up in the brewery, in real estate and the, and the brewery.
So until the brewery was sold or unless the brewery was sold, there was no way for us to pay that tax.
Because the tax was of a very high percentage, and our money, as I said, was tied up in that business.
So we found it impossible to get our passports and to get our visas and leave the country [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until the, until Kristallnacht.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But one thing I have to mention.
Through an inextricable error of the German authorities, my father and I got our passports back.
They took the passports away, must have been in 1938.

And in-- about six months later, we got our-- my father and I, not my mother and Hella, got our passports back, which was an out-and-out error, because you did not get your passport until the, that tax was paid, which we couldn't.

The minute we got our passports, we-- my father went to the Swiss consulate and got himself a Swiss transit visa, and I went to the French consulate and got a French transit visa, which I was able to get because we had business connections because of our hops business with France.
Hops are grown in the Alsace region of France.
Alsace is in, really, the, the, the border region between Germany and France.
It changed hands constantly between Germany and France, depending on who won the last war.
And so I got my French visa in my passport.
The French were not very friendly towards Jews and, generally speaking, did not issue visas.
Well, I was able to prove that we had business connections with France, and so they gave me the visa, a limited transit visa, not an immigration visa, a transit visa.
Yes.
A lot of them had left already.
Some were smart enough, enough to leave early.
And others-- some left illegally overnight, made their way across the border somehow.
There were people, undoubtedly Germans, who for a nice consideration would take them across the border, especially across the German-Dutch border.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the ranks had thinned already by that time.
